channel.title: HOME on Kanishak Vaidya PhD Progress
channel.description: Recent content in HOME on Kanishak Vaidya PhD Progress

item.title: Publications
item.description: Conferences Kanishak Vaidya and B. Sundar Rajan, ``Private Information Delivery with Coded Storage,'' 2022 - 2022 IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory (ISIT 2022), Espoo, Finland, 24 June – 2 July 2022. [IEEE] Kanishak Vaidya and B. Sundar Rajan, ``Distributed Computation: Privacy, Straggler Mitigation, and Security Against Colluding Workers,'' 2020 - 2020 IEEE Global Communications Conference, 2020, pp. 1-6, doi: 10.1109/GLOBECOM42002.2020.9322092. [IEEE] Workshops Kanishak Vaidya and B. Sundar Rajan, ``Cache-Aided Multi-Access Multi-User Private Information Retrieval,'' in Wi-Opt CCDWN 2022, Turin, Italy, 19 – 23 September 2022.

item.title: M.Tech Projects
item.description:
• Image segmentation using watershed transform
  • Image mosaic using homography estimation
  • Computer vision: Finding rotation and translation direction using two images

item.title: B.Tech Projects
item.description:
• Tow-wheel self balancing robot
  • plant self care system
  • Pizza delivery system
  • Gesture controlled wheelchair
  • Home security alarm

item.title: BIO
item.description: About Kanishak Vaidya KANISHAK VAIDYA DOB: 10 December, 1995 Email: kanishakvaidya@gmail.com Address: IISc Bangalore, CV Raman Rd, Bengaluru, Karnataka. 560012 Download RESUME Academic Background Kanishak Vaidya received the B.Tech. degree in Electronics and Communication Engineering from JawaharLal Nehru Government Engineering College, Sundernagar, HP India in 2017. He joined IISc Bangalore for M.Tech is Signal Processing in 2018 and is currently working towards the PhD degree with the Department of Electrical Communication Engineering, IISc Bangalore, India.

item.title: Private Information Delivery
item.description: Suppose a company outsources some of its user activity log data externally for statistical analysis, while it does not wish to reveal sensitive information about the user identities (e.g., names, addresses, groups). Or a hospital wants to send the medical record of a patient externally (e.g., for analysis of certain disease that goes beyond the capability of the current hospital), and it is desirable that the name of the patient is not revealed (i.e., the privacy of the patient is preserved). Such problems comes under the umbrella of private information delivery (PID).

item.title: Private Information Retrieval
item.description: Consider an investor that queries the stock-market database for the value of a certain stock may wish to keep private the identity of the stock he is interested in. Private information retrieval (PIR) is a protocol that allows a user to retrieve an item from distributed servers, in possession of a database without revealing which item is retrieved.

item.title: Coded Caching
item.description: The high temporal variability of network traffic results in communication systems that are congested during peak-traffic times and underutilized during off-peak times. One approach to reduce peak traffic is to take advantage of memories distributed across the network (at end users, routers, …) to duplicate content. This duplication of content, called content placement or caching, is performed during off-peak hours when network resources are abundant. During peak hours, when network resources are scarce, user requests can then be served exploiting these caches to reduce network congestion.

item.title: Distributed Computing
item.description: In distributed computation system, a master performs a computation by distributing it into smaller sub-computations and each sub-computation is performed by a worker node. Slow workers also known as stragglers nodes could become a bottleneck for the computation. As master's data is being shared by external workers, the privacy of data and master's behavior (demand patterns etc.) is also a matter of concern. Also distributed computations are much more susceptible to erroneous communication or malicious workers that may send arbitrary data to affect the computation. While these constraints are building upon distributed computing framework, low upload and download costs are preferred. Coding theoretic techniques are being used to overcome these and other challenges in distributed computation.
